title: Reading: A Family Dispute
learningGoal: Analyze a dialogue to identify formality levels (Tu, Tum, Aap) and track possessive pronouns.
instructions: Read the following family dialogue. Notice how Rahul speaks to his grandfather (Aap), his brother (Tum), and how the grandfather speaks to his grandson (Tu/Tum). Watch the possessive pronouns closely.
readingText:
राहुल: दादाजी, आप कैसे हैं? आपकी तबीयत कैसी है?
दादाजी: मैं ठीक हूँ बेटा। तुम्हारी पढ़ाई कैसी है?
राहुल: मेरी पढ़ाई अच्छी है। दादाजी, यह मेरी किताब है।
(राहुल का भाई, अमित, आता है।)
राहुल: अमित, तुम यहाँ क्या कर रहे हो? यह तेरी किताब नहीं है!
अमित: नहीं, यह मेरी किताब है! उसकी किताब वहाँ है।
दादाजी: बच्चो, लड़ो मत। तुम दोनों मेरे पोते हो।
textInTargetLanguage:
राहुल: दादाजी, आप कैसे हैं? आपकी तबीयत कैसी है?
दादाजी: मैं ठीक हूँ बेटा। तुम्हारी पढ़ाई कैसी है?
राहुल: मेरी पढ़ाई अच्छी है। दादाजी, यह मेरी किताब है।
(राहुल का भाई, अमित, आता है।)
राहुल: अमित, तुम यहाँ क्या कर रहे हो? यह तेरी किताब नहीं है!
अमित: नहीं, यह मेरी किताब है! उसकी किताब वहाँ है।
दादाजी: बच्चो, लड़ो मत। तुम दोनों मेरे पोते हो।
baseLanguageSupportText:
Rahul: Grandfather, how are you (Aap)? How is your (Aapki) health?
Grandfather: I am fine son. How is your (Tumhari) study?
Rahul: My (Meri) study is good. Grandfather, this is my (Meri) book.
(Rahul's brother, Amit, comes.)
Rahul: Amit, what are you (Tum) doing here? This is not your (Teri - intimate/angry) book!
Amit: No, this is my (Meri) book! His (Uski) book is there.
Grandfather: Children, don't fight. You both are my (Mere - plural) grandsons.

comprehensionQuestions.questionText: Why does Rahul use 'Aapki' when asking about his grandfather's health (tabiyat)?
options: Because he respects his grandfather, and 'tabiyat' is feminine.

comprehensionQuestions.questionText: Why does the grandfather use 'Tumhari' when asking about Rahul's studies (padhai)?
options: He is speaking casually to his grandson, and 'padhai' is feminine.

comprehensionQuestions.questionText: When Rahul gets annoyed at his brother Amit, he drops his formality level. What word does he use for 'your'?
Amit says 'Uski kitaab wahaan hai' (His book is there). To whom does 'Uski' refer?
Why does the grandfather say 'Mere pote' (My grandsons) instead of 'Mera pote'?
options: Because 'pote' (grandsons) is plural masculine, so 'mera' becomes 'mere'.

correctAnswers.rq_1: Because he respects his grandfather, and 'tabiyat' is feminine.
correctAnswers.rq_2: He is speaking casually to his grandson, and 'padhai' is feminine.
correctAnswers.rq_5: Because 'pote' (grandsons) is plural masculine, so 'mera' becomes 'mere'.

expectedSummaryPoints: Understand how formality shifts based on relationship.
Recognize that possessives ALWAYS match the object (Kitaab, Tabiyat = feminine), not the speaker.

feedbackRules.onPass: Great job! You successfully analyzed the complex social layers of this conversation.
feedbackRules.onFail: Review Section 2. The possessive pronoun must match both the Formality Level (Aap/Tum/Tu) AND the Gender of the object.
feedbackRules.onPartial: Good effort. Note how Rahul shifts from 'Tum' to 'Teri' when he gets angry at his brother. This is a very common use of 'Tu'.

audioAssets.audioTranscript: राहुल: दादाजी, आप कैसे हैं? आपकी तबीयत कैसी है? दादाजी: मैं ठीक हूँ बेटा। तुम्हारी पढ़ाई कैसी है? राहुल: मेरी पढ़ाई अच्छी है। दादाजी, यह मेरी किताब है। राहुल: अमित, तुम यहाँ क्या कर रहे हो? यह तेरी किताब नहीं है! अमित: नहीं, यह मेरी किताब है! उसकी किताब वहाँ है। दादाजी: बच्चो, लड़ो मत। तुम दोनों मेरे पोते हो।

hintText: Notice how all the feminine objects (health, study, book) force the possessive pronoun to end in 'i'. Aapki, Meri, Tumhari, Teri, Uski.
commonMistakes: Thinking 'Aapki' implies the grandfather is feminine.
